Okay, so I know we went a little bit deep right there, but thank you guys for letting me geek out. This is the stuff I think about all night long. So I just wanted to share that stuff with you because it is important, especially as we start doing all the other stuff. So we're gonna come out and have a little more fun now for the next couple of presentations. But hopefully that'll set a really good foundation to understand how we're structuring the content, where we're finding people, meeting them more than that, and having messages that speak directly to them and bring people back in the funnel. So that's what we kind of went through all that groundwork. What we're gonna do right now is, I've got about an hour till lunch, I wanna go through some cool things. So the biggest goal is after somebody, after somebody goes through your funnel, and they're added to your list, to your world, you've got a really cool chance to build a relationship with that person. If you build that relationship right, people will continue to buy from you over and over and over and over again. I'm sure you've heard a million times, it's way easier to keep an existing customer happy than it is to keep getting new customers all day long. I've had some businesses where all our goal is to get new customers all day long, and those businesses wear you out really, really fast. And so we wanna transition to businesses where we can get people to come and keep on giving us money. I read twice this week the article, it's called A Thousand True Fans. Have any of you guys read that article? It's worth reading. It's a little techy. This guy's not like a storyteller, but it's a really cool concept. And he basically said, he said that what a true fan is, is someone who's willing to spend one day of their income towards you every single year. So someone makes 100 bucks a day, that every year they're willing to invest $100 to you. He's kinda talking more about artists and things like that. He says that a true fan is someone who, anything you come out with, they buy. And if you're doing an event, they're gonna come, and they're gonna pay for backstage passes. They're obsessed, they wanna be around everything. Those are true fans. It says that the point where you get a thousand true fans in your business is the point where you're free. If you have a thousand true fans each paying you 100 bucks a month, or whatever, that's $100,000 a month, whatever that is. But a thousand true fans sets you free. And if you have a thousand true fans, it means you've probably got 10,000 warm fans, and then you got the cold masses. And so I look at this whole business as that's the goal. Here in the middle, we've got our true fans. I want as many people here as possible. Out here is our warm people. You see what I'm hand-writing, right? It says warm, and out here is our cold, right? So a whole funnel of catchers, bringing people from cold, bring them through here, and then eventually having them here in our true fans. How many guys in your business right now know that you've got true fans who are obsessed with what you're doing and selling? That's where we all want to be at, right? And if you're there, we want more. If you're not there yet, we want some. So that's kind of the goal. So I want to go through just some things. I was thinking about last night, how do we create true fans? Different than just customers. Because customers are nice, but if you want fans, people who will go to bat for you, people who will stick them in forms and yell at other people for you, right? People that'll keep buying your stuff. How do you cultivate true fans? And a couple years ago, I read a whole bunch of these books and stuff on how to start a cult, which they're really good books. My wife was like, what are you? What's the point of you reading these books? I'm like, no, this is really good. But talking about, in fact, one of my friends wrote a creative product called ethical cult building. And it's all about how do you get people to come in and to become true fans and become part of your vision and your dream and what you're doing. And so it's kind of interesting. So these are just some of the things that, as I was kind of going through it, thinking through it, that I think are the keys, right? Is to get these true fans in. So step number one is your attractive character. As you've been in Russell Brunson world for more than like 50 minutes, you've probably heard me talk a lot about attractive character. So I'm not gonna go and spend three hours going through that. But I'm curious, how many guys feel like you have identified and defined your attractive character? How many guys feel like you've done that so far? Okay. On the other side, how many of you guys think that your audience is aware of most of these things inside your attractive character? A couple, very small. So I've been talking, it's funny, there's be a couple, you heard me rant a couple times this weekend about stuff that I've been preaching from up here for two years over and over. The two biggest ones are probably attractive character and dream 100. Like how many of you guys heard me talk about dream 100 at least 100 times? How many of you guys have done the dream 100 yet? All right, a couple, good, good, good. So attractive character is important. So again, I'm not gonna spend a ton of time on this aspect because there's trainings in the Ignite members area you guys can go through where I go for hours just talking about this and totally geeking out on it. But it's key. Like when I first learned about this, I learned from John Alliness who's in the pick up, how to pick up chicks market, right? How to get girls to come to you. And he talked all about like, he was in a business event, he said, look, I'm gonna talk to you entrepreneurs the same way I talked to my men who were trying to get girls to come to him, right? He said, because the laws of attraction are the same. If you want women to come to you, you've gotta be an attractive person. I'm not like attractive, like I'm a good looking person. Like attractive, there's qualities and traits in people that attract other people, right? And he said the same thing is true in business. Like you've gotta put things out there that draw people in and get them excited and make them want to be part of your world, right? And so this is kind of a little thing I wrote out to kind of sketch out. Like what are the important things in an attractive character, right? Every good attractive character has got a good backstory. Okay, how many of your fans know your backstory? Okay, if not, it's time to start sharing that stuff. Podcasting, video, like I share, how many of you guys have heard way too many stories from me? Yeah, like I share my stories over, so backstories, parables to help teach important concepts. Like what parables you have that you can share with people? What character flaws do you let people see about yourself? What kind of, how are you polar, like these things like that? If you haven't started doing this yet, I really wanna use this kind of wake up call to think about it, because this has everything to do with how much money you're gonna make. As nothing is getting cold traffic in, it has everything to do with taking these cold traffic people and getting them to want to buy into you and your vision and what you're doing and to give you money over and over and over again. So it's important. So step number one is really identifying and focusing on your attractive character. Couple of fun things we're doing, just off of this, right? One of the story lines here is us versus them. Who has sent an email or done a podcast or something, us versus them with your audience? A couple. So we send this out to 400 people two weeks ago and it's us versus them. Have you guys ever heard the webinar where people always ask like, what's the difference between you guys and lead pages? Like it does math, you can do addition, subtraction, it's awesome at doing math. And ClickFunnels is kind of like an iPhone, like we've got a calculator app, but we do a whole bunch of other stuff as well, right? That's kind of messing with them. And so thinking like, how do we do us versus them without like actually saying lead pages with kind of poking at them? So we did us versus them, it's like a pairing calculator to a smartphone and it's a shirt and it's awesome. And the best, it doesn't actually say lead pages. Le, D, P, A, G, E, F. So, all right. So what we're doing, we're doing a really fun campaign where we got a guy who's doing print on demand, he's doing print on demand shirts, right? So you pay someone a hundred bucks to design a t-shirt and then you can put the widget into ClickFunnels, someone comes and basically we're just free plus shipping on the shirts, they order it, they can pick a color or size, they can do hoodies, they can do like a whole bunch of stuff, they pick what they want, click submit, and then they cover shipping handling and we mail them out their t-shirt and there's a guy that prints them and ships them in it. It's really cool. So we'll have that for you guys to be able to play with soon, but anyway, so we're gonna be doing a new campaign with that and trying to get everybody, all of our TrueFans wearing, how many of you guys right now are wearing a ClickFunnels or a Dotcom Series t-shirt? Right now, you're not wearing right now. Who's going on right now? Couple, we got two or three, all right. So we got a couple of TrueFans. I was telling them, our mastermind group yesterday, we are in our, one of my older groups four or five years ago, we had this guy's name was Big Mike, big burly man, and he owns the biggest hydroponics company in the world, and does like 80 or $90 million a year back then, I don't know what they're now. And anyway, every year they do these big national conventions, and he wanted to go buy a booth at the convention, so he goes and tries to buy a booth and they're like, no, we hate you because you're the biggest guy and you're kind of, he totally like this, like just, anyway, he personifies the stuff we're talking about, like big attractive character, like loud and annoying and awesome and his competitors all hate him. And so we went to sell him a booth, they wouldn't even sell him tickets to the event, so he was pissed. And so we did the strategy session in our mastermind group, and I thought it was like a joke as we went through his turn, and then he actually did everything that we kind of made up. So this is what he did, first thing he did is he made this t-shirt and had big muscular bowl on it, and the bowl is drinking his product like this, you see the name of the product on the jug, and then the bowl is peeing out into a bucket, and it's got his competitor's product name on the bucket. And so the night before the event, he rented out across the street this huge old thing, brought in girls and all these people, and he threw his party for the whole convention, 5,000 people, come to the convention, I say come to this party, he gave everyone a free t-shirt, so every single person had one, he said, for the next four days we got spies and going around the convention, each have like 40 or 50 grand in $100 bills, and if you're running a show, they see they'll give you $100 bills. So for the next three days of the event, every single person was wearing that, and the entire, like blanketed the whole thing, and then, you think that was enough, right? So the next thing he did, just to piss him off even more, is he rented a helicopter with a big old banner out of that picture on it, and for three days he flew around in a circle above the convention center. It was like the coolest thing in the world. Anyway, he made his point, and he won. All right, this is another shirt we're coming out with, it's kind of funny. Um. So we'll have that shirt for you guys too, if you guys are interested. All right, so doing cool stuff like that, doing stuff that people bond to, right? Like I want all you guys to wear when we're not Confusionsoft t-shirts, so wouldn't that be awesome? Anyway, except for the Confusionsoft certified people in the room who are like angry at me right now, but it's okay if you're upset, I'll get over it. All right, step number two. Not only do you have to be like an attractive character, you have to be a master at your craft. I cannot stress this enough. Okay, you have to become amazing at what you do and what you know. Okay, you've gotta be amazing. If you're not amazing, nobody's gonna become a true fan. Okay, so a couple of things with this. First off, and I talk about this a lot, you've gotta get results. You gotta go out there and find people, serve them, work for free for them, and get results. That's what proves you know what you're talking about. Okay, that's a big key. Next thing is, if you're gonna master your craft, I think the first time I really got this, how many of you guys know Howard Berg, the world's fastest reader? I've seen some stuff we did with him. So I met Howard the first time. I saw, when I was like 12 years old, I saw his infomercials online, and he's reading books like this, and he read a whole book in like three minutes. It was crazy. And one day I'm in an event, and I get on speaking and I walk off, and some guy's like, oh, the world's fastest reader wants to meet you. I'm like, Howard Berg? Like, you know who he is? I'm like, yeah. So I go meet this guy, he's a little short guy, and I start talking and just hit it off, super cool. He's been in our office like 10 times. He trained me how to speed read our whole staff, and the guy's just awesome. And I remember us talking about some concepts, and, because the guy's, he's read over 30,000 books. He knows everything on earth about everything, because he can read a book in like five minutes. So he's just like, oh, I wanna learn that. And the most interesting thing I learned from him that really meant a lot to me, he said most people, they read a book, and then they take that as like the perception of the world revolves around like that author's opinion of the thing. He says that's like the wrong way to learn. He says, what you gotta do is, you gotta take like seven books on a topic, because everyone's got different things. And you look at all seven of these, and all of these, instead of just seeing like the sliver of the universe, you see like the real picture of it. Okay? And when I learned, when he said that, I was just like, man, like how many things in my life am I like looking at just one little piece of it? And I'm basing my reality, and the reality I'm sharing with my students and other people I'm trying to serve on this one little sliver of information. Like that's, like I'm not serving myself or other people. So I started, I took that to heart. So when I started like learning topics, like I remember when I kind of, I went to Tony Robbins' event the first time, and I heard about NLP, and I was like, man, this is a cool thing, like how does this work? I wanna say like how I can use it and everything. And so what I did is I took Howard's advice. I went onto like Amazon, and went and bought every NLP book I could find like, I don't know, like 10 or 15 of them. Like tons of them came to me. And then I was like online, trying to find some audio courses and books and bunch of stuff. I got all of it in, you know, to come to me. And I said, I'm gonna do seven days of immersion. Like just for a week, where this is what I'm gonna focus on. I'm gonna just like, just cram and just figure everything out. And so I went through and I obviously, not as fast as Howard Berg. But in a week of time of focus, like concentration, you can learn a lot of stuff. And I went through, I was reading his books, I'm skipping parts of reading as fast as I could. I'm listening to audios, I'm like immersing myself in this concept. It was interesting when you, when you do that, when you immerse yourself in a concept for a finite period of time, all these like weird connections start connecting that wouldn't normally, you know what I mean? That you just start getting, cause you're like, wow, you see this and this. And also like this, this full picture opens up of like what reality actually is. Okay. And I think a lot of times people who are coaches are like, I've got one of my really close friends. He's a personal trainer and he's not here. So he probably won't watch the videos and if he is, I apologize, but he needs to hear this too. So he, he's very like a traditional trainer. And he's gone through like one school of thought and he's good at what he does. But the problem is like he only respects and understands this one school of thought. And so like when I came to him and started working, I'm like, he was able to do certain things, but, but everybody's different, right? Every situation is different. And so like I was stuck in this thing. I honestly wasn't talking about, where's Anthony at? Is he still in the room? Like Anthony is like the opposite side of that, right? Like he's a trainer who like understands like a thousand, someone who has mastered this craft, who's gone through so much immersion, understands, well, this is this type of thing. And this type, all these type of things. And then coming to me saying, well, based on your situation, this is what we should try to do. And suddenly I started getting results from that, right? And so if you want to serve your students at the best spot, the biggest thing I can say is like, you've got to become a master of your craft. You got to stop focusing on just like what you know and find out like the entire picture and like just immerse yourself for a week or two, whatever it takes just to become amazing because that's when people are going to get it, right? Like you guys can probably tell how much I geek out on this marketing stuff. Like that's all I, like I'm so obsessed. Like, cause it's so much fun and all these things I can pull out of it and use. If I was up here and I only knew one thing, you guys probably wouldn't be that excited. You probably wouldn't be here. But I don't know, when you're passionate and you're excited, other people get excited as well. So I'd say one of the biggest things about getting true fans is you just mastering your craft, focusing the time and the energy just to become amazing at it. I was talking to Troy last week about it. And Troy, I'm not sure if he's in the room or not, but he launched a new business recently. And I asked him, what? He said, you know, like it's been good, but it's been hard. And I asked one of the things that one of the hardest things is like, is I started getting clients and like, I didn't know how to serve some of these people. And I had to learn, I had to go through this huge learning process where I'm learning like insane amounts of stuff for a finite period of time to be able to figure out how to actually serve these people. It's like, now that I've got these new tools, like there's so much I can do, but I had to go through that immersion process to master my craft before I could ever serve correctly. I think that's a big key. And so I want to stress that. I know that some of you guys are thinking I'm crazy, but like, it's important. All right, step number three. I call this the prolific index, okay? Some of you guys may have seen me talk about this before. This is the prolific index, okay? In the middle here, we've got mainstream thoughts and ideas. Okay, and there's zero money in the mainstream. Okay, I went to college, they teach you the mainstream. Okay, which is why they don't pay teachers very much, right? The mainstream, there's no money in. Okay, I think about the health market, right? The four food groups, like there's no money in like, hey guys, guess what? I got this new food plan, it's called the four food groups. You got bread, breads, whatever, right? There's no money in the mainstream. So if what you're selling is the mainstream, like no one's gonna give you money. Okay, now the other side, there's the crazy, okay? And the crazy, people are also not gonna give you any money. Okay, but somewhere between the mainstream and the crazy is the prolific. That's the sweet spot. When you can find in your market the prolific, that's where people will start paying you money. That's where they're following you. Okay, so you gotta find out for you where that is. Okay, for example, we got four food groups here in the mainstream. We've got maybe like liposuction over here, like we'll just shave the fat off of your body over on the crazy side, right? In the middle, you got let's drink butter and our coffee. Right? It's kind of in the middle, you're like, well, like that's crazy, but like I can see it. And if it works, holy cow, I can drink butter every morning. Like, and boom. Okay, we hung out with Dave last month, he's making crazy money with this, right? So for you, you gotta figure out where that is. Okay, if nobody's buying your stuff, I'm guessing you're probably in the mainstream or you're in the looney bin, all right? So we gotta either come back a little bit this way or we gotta go a little bit that way, but somewhere between there, that's the sweet spot, okay? You think about like the podcast you listen to. Like which ones do you listen to? Is this the mainstream boring ones? You might listen to the crazy ones, right? I don't know. But this is where like our tensions at. This is what gets news coverage. This is what gets bloggers right about you. This is what gets people to click. Like that's the sweet spot of where all the magic happens. Okay, so shifting yourself from the mainstream and find the prolific zone. Like that's a key. That's what gets people coming back to you over and over and over again, okay? So I want to show you that because I was talking to Gary about this. And I was like, hey man, I saw your old videos. He's like, oh man. He's like, I used to put those out when nobody ever watched them. He's like, I did like 130 or 140 episodes. I was like, why did you stop? He's like, I was trying to learn my voice. Because I know what people respond to. I was trying this, I was trying that. I was trying the crazy. And eventually find out the sweet spot where people are like, wow, I respond to that. Like that resonates with me, right? And when you find that out, then you can create things towards that, right? It's interesting as we filmed Tony Robbins, Dave Ashbury, Neil Strauss. I filmed myself a lot. Look at, with Neil, his voice is different. And the way we capture things were different than Dave's or mine. And so you got to figure out what your voice is. And the only way to do that is to practice it over and over and over and over and over and over again. And that's something that's usually not very fun at first, right? It doesn't make you much money at first. But if you want to master anything, like you got to really become good at it. But you think about like, how many in here know me in my personal life, like outside of Russell Brunson, you're a marketer? You can ask my staff, like, I'm not me. I can't realize. I go to church on Sunday to hide in the back from everyone. I'm like scared to death. Like it's interesting. Like Russell versus like my voice to my audience is way different. My wife comes these things and she's like, you are so weird on stage. You talk a lot and you're like, oh, animate it. And she's like, it's not you. And I'm like, I know. It's not that it's not genuine. Like it's me. But it's me in a way that people respond to, right? And so I think that you can do both. But for me, it's outside of my comfort zone. If I was to go to church and talk like this, I'd not feel comfortable, right? My home is just different. So I think just find out your voice and find what people do respond to. But in a way that is authentic to you, that's fun that you still enjoy. Because if you don't enjoy it, you're not going to do it, right? And again, it doesn't have to be video either. Like I like video. Garrett likes video. Some of you guys, how many of you guys run podcasts in here? Some podcast is your voice. Like that's the thing. Some of you guys are writers in the room. Some of you guys write. Like it was actually interesting. Nils Strauss was horrible on camera. I think one of the best writers I've ever read. But like one of the worst guys on camera, right? And so you got to find out not only your voice, but where your message should come from. Like what's the platform that you're comfortable with? Like what do you like doing? How do you like speaking? But it's something you got to learn. You got to learn how to write. You got to learn how to speak. Whatever your craft is, whatever that thing is, you got to really master that. And it comes from like tons and tons and tons of practice. All right, step number five is polarity. Nobody follows the neutral person. There's no value. There's no excitement. There's nothing in there. This is one of my amazing graphs to illustrate my points. So this is the neutral graph, right? Where you're just neutral. Like everything's happy and you don't offend anybody. You're like, OK, everything's perfect. And when you're neutral, like nobody seems to care. How many of you guys are watching the presidential election right now? This is the first time I have ever in the history of my life seen a presidential debate, ever. Literally, I don't follow it at all. Last election, I asked Brent, who was not an American citizen, time like, hey man, I'm not going to vote. So who do you want me to vote for? He's into it, right? So I just let him vote for me, because I don't even know. This time, I'm engaged because there's this dude on there who is so freaking polar that if I miss the debates the next morning, it's all over Facebook. I'm watching this dude kick Ron Paul or Rand Paul. What if I offstage because he doesn't have any? I'm watching this stuff like a train wreck. And I'm probably going to vote this year on my own. I don't even know who for, but I care because I'm into this thing now, right? How many of us feel a little bit like that? Whether you love him or hate him, I'm talking about obviously don't trust me. I don't care if you love me or you hate him. What happens is there's polarity, right? And you may be on the side that hates him, which is cool. I don't really care. Only thing I care about is those little lines right there where the money is made, right? He's getting tons of the voters. Even people he's deeply offending are following him now, right? Because then, I don't know if it's going to last. He may crash and burn. He may be going off the prolific zone to the crazy land. I don't know. I'm not into that kind of stuff. I don't know yet. We'll see. But there's value in that if you understand that, right? By being the neutral person who's just in the middle, nobody will listen. They won't respond. They're not going to care. By being polar, that's how you get attention and interest and people who actually care. And by doing that, you're going to get a group of people who love you and a group of people who hate you. And it's hard. It's painful. I remember the very first time my wife Googled my name. And she's like, all these people are calling you a scam artist. Is that true? I'm like, no. But there's people that don't like me. It just happens. And it's hard, but you've got to become OK with that. Because the way to create true fans, there has to be polarity. And percentage of people are going to hate you. But because that happens, there's a percentage that hate you and then there's a percentage that loves you. And the percentage that loves you, those are your true fans. Those are the people who wear your t-shirts, who show up to your events, who buy your stuff, who go on every tele-seminar, every periscope, everything you do, because they love you. You don't get that by being neutral. If you look at Pepsi versus Coke, Mac versus PC, if you look at history the way it works, this is me geeking out on branding. So if you guys zone out, but I think it's fun. So when PC was the number, when they were the best, Apple came in with their campaign, Mac versus PC, because they were number two. And if you notice that since Apple has passed Mac, have you seen a single Mac commercial talking about PC? Because number two cannot acknowledge, or excuse me, number one cannot acknowledge number two, or it hurts their positioning and their branding. Coke versus Pepsi. Pepsi's always yelling, Coke never talks about it. So when we came out, these pages are ahead of us. They still are. We'll pass them this year. So right now we're making a bunch of, making fun of them. But we'll pass them soon and we'll quit talking about them. Same thing with Infusionsoft. That's our next target. So we'll go after them and eventually we'll start talking. So two can talk about one, but one, you can't talk about them. So with your competitors, it depends. If someone's below you, I'd never talk about 10 minute pages all of their crap. Because if I talk about them, I validate that they're alive. And I don't want to do that. So I don't ever talk about them. So I only talk about people that we're trying to be. So for you, it depends on your positioning. If you're on number one in your market, don't talk about anybody else. You've got to figure out who's the common enemy. With these guys, it's the gun grabbing, whatever people. That's their common enemy that the rest of their group hates. So it can be something like that. I know John Allness does it with doers versus refunders. That's his whole message. Like, hey, you a doer or a refunder? And people ask for refunds. Oh, I see you're not a doer. Everyone's scared. And then he calls them out. No one dares to refund from him because they want to be doers. So they're all doers. And so it just depends on what is that in your market that the common enemy that people all see. It might be a competitor. A type of customer. It might be whatever. So it depends. Step number six. And this is different than the content we'll be talking about after lunch. So after lunch, we're talking about creating content to bring cold people into your world. This, excuse me, this is different. This is how do we communicate with our fans? How do we get these guys excited and fired up? So many of us spend so much, I'm guilty as well. We spend so much time trying to figure out how to do customers. You do customers in the door to forget about our existing customers. It's hard because that's what's on your mind all the time. And so I've been trying to figure out like how do I keep communication with my fans so they're always excited and interested and engaged all the time. And email is obviously a big one. I think everyone has to do email. If you're not doing email, that's got to be a key. For you or for any business, you've got to be doing email. And the next one is you've got to figure out what platform you're comfortable with. Those of you guys who are bloggers, you should be blogging because people are going to read your blog and follow and be engaged. If you're not a blogger, don't do that because it's not congruent. It's hard. And you've got to find a platform that's not hard for you. If it's hard, you're not going to do it. I know I tried to do a podcast for eight years, and I never did until I got my marketing in your car and it was easy for me, so now I do it. So blogging, podcasting, and Periscope. Periscope is my new favorite channel. How many of you guys have been on one of my Periscopes so far? It's awesome. What's that? You were the first Periscope I ever went on, by the way. I was driving my car, my phone went bleep bleep bleep bleep. And I was like, huh, and I clicked on it. And she was live. I was so excited. Probably threw you off high. It was so exciting. I was on Noah's first one. I want to get all your guys' Periscope. So I'm going to sell you on Periscope for a few minutes here for a couple of reasons. First off, I love email. When I first got started in this business, my first coach was Mark Joyner. And he pounded in my head, like, the only thing that matters is building a list. And so I was like, all right. I was too dumb to know anything else. I just focused on building a list. It served me for over a decade. Building a list, you can sell these guys' products. And then all these other things have come and gone, like Facebook and Twitter and all these things. And all of them have been good, but it's like, with email, I send an email and you each get it. At Facebook, I post a thing. They got to write ads. And it's not a direct communication channel with my audience, right? Periscope, the way I found out about it, honestly, someone told me that. So I downloaded it. I know what it was. And then a week later, I'm driving in my car to lunch. And Austin chirped at me. And it was Stacy. I was like, I love Stacy. So I clicked on it. I'm watching her driving. And I was like, this is the coolest thing. I didn't get it. And people were clicking hearts. I was like, click on it. I'm like, oh, and I'm clicking little hearts on it. I was like, it was really engaging. I was like, this is awesome. And so then I forgot about it. Then a week or two later, I was playing with it again. I'm scrolling around. And I accidentally clicked the broadcast button. And I didn't know what this was or how it worked. And all of a sudden, it's like, something's all popped up. And the next person, within a minute, I had 20 people on there. And I was like, I don't know who these people are. I don't know how they heard it. I was kind of freaking out. And then I was like, hey, everyone, this is my first Periscope. I don't know what's happening. But this is so exciting. And then I kind of ended it. I was like, OK, that was interesting. I clicked a button. Instantly, I was there with people. Only other channel I've ever had that with ever has been email. Like, podcast is not that way. I do a podcast. And some people see it, some people don't. It's kind of random. Blogging, saying I put a blog post, some people do, some people don't. When I do a Periscope, all your phones go bleep, bleep, bleep, and Russell's live. And then you're there instantly. Now with email, I think about a good email. If I send out a good email, I might get 5,000 clicks. On a great email, I get 10,000 clicks. That's kind of my metrics. If I get 10,000 clicks, it's going to be awesome. With Periscope, I did a Periscope last night at 9.30. How many of you guys were on that one last night? So I clicked that button. When less than a minute, we had 100 people live. Within a minute in, we had 200 people live at 9.30 at night who were on the phone version. And then we had another, I can look at my stats, another 250 that were watching the web-based version. I had 500 people instantly communicating with me. I could have sold something, I could have talked about it. I was instant communication with my true fans, instantly. And anyway, it's interesting. And so for me, I've only been doing this for a couple weeks. And I'm going to put a lot of time and effort in this, because right now I got 200 people live that fast. In six months, I may have 2,000 people. In a year, I may have 20,000 people. In five years, I may have 200,000 people. Right click a button, and instantly 200,000 people are sitting there talking to me. For me, this is the first thing besides email I've ever been that excited about. So a couple of things I want to stress about this. First off, like I said, it's the first direct communication channel besides email. I love it. I think it's better than email, because I can get, instead of 5,000 clicks, I can get 5,000 people that I'm talking to. The second thing is, it's kind of hard to get people to follow you. And so because of that, not many people are really doing it. We're emailing, everyone's been emailing now. So email inbox to get more. I follow probably 100 people on my periscope. And I've maybe had three people that have ever broadcasted live. So it's like, there's not much noise there. I don't think there's going to be a lot of noise, because I don't think it's easy. I think it's going to be harder. I look at, in fact, I'm not sure if he's in the room. I'm just blanked out his name. I did Decade Day last week with him. He has the real estate podcast for nine years. OK, there you are. So nine years ago, everyone's talking about podcasting. I heard about podcasts nine years ago. I'm like, hey, I'm going to do a podcast. And then I didn't. And then I didn't. And then I didn't. So he was smarter than me. He was listening. So I'm going to do a podcast. We started doing a podcast. We do that three times a week. OK, three times a week. Newest podcast. And on our Decade Day call, I'm like, OK, so talk about your business. I got this podcast. I'm doing it for nine years. All these people love me. All these downloads and everything. I'm like, OK, where do you send people to? And he's like, well, I don't really have any funnels. I'm like, OK, well, how do you do these events? How do you do people do events? I'm like, I just tell them to come to the event and they show up. I'm like, OK. I'm like, well, OK. And then he's got some home study course. How do you sell it? He's like, well, there's a picture in the button and then you just buy it. I'm like, OK, well, I'm trying to figure this out. And so in my mind, I'm like, he's got to be completely broke because there's no way people are buying from this. That's kind of my mindset. And I'm like, what were your revenue last year? It's like over $2 million. I'm like, he doesn't have a sales funnel or anything, but he's got tons of true fans who are obsessed with him. And it's because he put in insane amounts of time and effort consistently for nine years. So I wish nine years ago I would have started my podcast and done it consistently because I'd be making a crap millions and millions of dollars from doing that. So right now, Periscope's this new thing. This is podcasting nine years ago. I think anybody, and I was sitting in a room like this nine years ago because I'm talking about podcasting, I'm like, there's such nerds. No one's listening to a podcast. I don't even have a microphone. God, I was stupid. I think this is the next thing. And I think that everyone of you guys should be Periscope. So that's number one. Number two is what is the best way for you to learn your voice? Doing it. A lot. Periscope is so easy for me to consist, because I just do it. I click a button, I talk five minutes, and I hang up. And I just do it over and over. I think in the last three weeks, I've done like 30 something episodes. And I'm learning my voice, and I'm seeing what people respond to. And you get instant feedback. If they click the little thing, then you know that something's good. If they don't, you don't. Also, you get feedback on who shows up. Every day I get to write a new headline. And I get to see, OK, how many people actually showed up for this one? Like last night, that was the fastest I had people on, and the longest, or excuse me, the most people. And part of it's growing, part of it's from the head lines. What headlines people respond to faster, which ones are slower? What's my audience actually care about? That they're going to at 9.30 tonight click on a button to list me talk about. I remember when I was talking to Jeff Walker, when we won the Ferrari, we were hanging out the day before. And I was talking about his, when he got in this business, he said the biggest thing for him is I learned I'd have to publish. He's like initially when I started, I had to list like six people, and most of them were related to me. And he's like, so I started publishing, started putting things out there. And I started slowly growing by putting things out there just to see what people responded to. It's like 90% of the stuff I send out there, nobody cared. And people thought I was really passionate about it, but nobody cared. Nobody cared. Nobody cared. Nobody cared. Nobody cared. Product launch. Wow, everyone got really excited about that. In fact, I had a product before Jeff had a product launch formula called pre-launch secrets that did awesome. And then a year later, Jeff Walker came out with product launch secrets. And that became a secret. It's like 10 years, he does like $10 million every year to sell the same product every single year. I got the same feedback when I did my pre-launch secrets, and I didn't listen. And I just walked off, and I was stupid. Well, he's just built this huge business off of the one thing that people responded to. And you don't know that until you put things out there. And so it gives you the opportunity to put a ton of things out there, learn your voice, see what people responded to. And initially, it's going to be like a shock over you. You're shooting stuff out in the sky, hoping things happen. Eventually, you start hitting things. You're like, wow, people showed up, participated on that topic. I just must care about that. Let me do a little more on that. A little more on that. And soon you're going to find out not only your voice, but what your message is. What it is that people care about for you. And again, you guys can do this through email, through blog, through podcast. But I also recommend adding, at least adding periscope, if not also doing it. But I think those of us who put in the time today, in a year from now, five years from now, it's going to be insane. Imagine right now, how many cable channels do you think it's two in the morning have 10,000 people watching them? We're all going to be able in a year from now. Click a button, and have 10,000 people on that fast. That's power. Yeah? So what are you doing? So this question is like, what are you doing to periscope content? So right now, you do it, and the periscope saves it for a day, and then it's gone. So I just have my brother whenever I'm done. It saves on your phone. I just forward that video to him, and then he goes to periscope and like, camptages the video so you can see all the hearts in the comments, and then plugs my video in, and put it on YouTube. So I don't know what I'm going to do right now. We're saving them all just in case. There's something called catch that saves them. I've never done that, but I think you do like hashtag catch, and then they save them, right? So there's a couple ways. And again, I don't know what I'm going to do with those yet. I know I'm going to. But what's cool is the other day we did one that had a really good response and engagement, stuff like that. And so it's on periscope for 24 hours. So John went and was like, hey, that one had way more engagement than anything else we've done so far. So he turned on Facebook ads to that. And all of a sudden, because we knew it got engagement for 24 hours, he was driving tons of ads, and we got more and more engagement. And it was like this little cherry bomb. He was like, boom, and it blew up and was awesome. And so it's kind of cool. You do it on this platform over here, and you find the ones that are hot, and you find that's hot, and go over to Facebook, and this is what we're talking about after lunch. You go to Facebook, promote it, and drive traffic to that. And again, it just gives you the ability to know what to do. Because a lot of times there's great content, and we're paying to promote it, and you're like, what if no one cares? This gives you the ability to kind of find out ahead of time. And it's fun. Anyway, so if you're not on periscope yet, you need to download, at least follow me, because I want to hang out with you guys every day. So you do that once a day, and it's super fun. My first 10 were 20 minutes long. And then I did one. I called it a marketing quickie, and it was like five minutes. And it was way better. And so all of a minor five minutes. In fact, I called it, and I called it the marketing quickie show now. So the way I do mine, there's a couple things. So you click start, and as soon as you do, it starts buzzing everybody. But first no one's there, so it's always awkward. You're like, talk. Do I not talk? Like, what do I do? No one's here. And then as soon as people start showing up, I'm like, hey, Joe, hey, Susie, hey, Mike. And you start seeing people here. I start communicating with them while they're coming in. And then what we've done the last 10 shows or so is I want to have at least 100 people on each one. So I'm like, hey, I'm going to share my thing with you guys, but we need to have at least 100 people who are not going to get started. And then what they can do is I tell people, hey, if you push the little thing over, it says share on Facebook or share on Twitter. They can click a button. It shares with all their fans and all their followers. And so the first minute, I'm getting everybody who's on there to share it with their people, which is like pushing this out, right? I got people coming all the time, like, hey, Julie told me to show up. Like, what is this about? And I'm like, we're talking about funnels. Like, what's a funnel? I'm like, crap. Cold market's coming out. I need to change my language patterns, right? But also, all these people sharing because they want me to teach the cool thing. Within a minute, we got 100 people on. And last night, within a minute and a half, we had over 200. It was just like, it was going crazy. And all these people were promoting. I'm telling them they're promoting. They're all doing it. And then you get ranked by hearts. And so for me, I just tell people to give me hearts. But you get fun. They click on it. If you had really fun, double click. And I get everyone, because Periscope ranks you based on how many hearts you've gotten. So last night's episode, we got almost 12,000 hearts last night. Because I got everyone like, I'm like, do double double, triple tap. And I'm like, slam your fingers on like crazy. So everyone's going crazy. And it's just like, it's awesome. And everyone loves it. And I can see afterwards, it shows you how many people send the most hearts. I'm like, man, so many people gave me like 4,000 hearts by yourself. That's awesome. So it's just fun platform. Again, I'm new to it still. But it's just, it's the first thing that gets me excited. Like, I can't wait until the day where I can just click a button and 10,000 people are on. Can you imagine the power in that? Like, it just changes. Even when 1,000, I mean, 1,000 true fans. Like, and they're there, engage every single day with you. How does that change the dynamic to your business? Yeah. For questions, do you give a call to action? So I don't yet. I probably will eventually. It's kind of like, you guys see the movie, The Social Network? So you know the part where they're at the sushi place and they're eating, and Justin Timberlake comes in. He's supposed to be, who's he playing? Yeah. So he comes in. And they're like, trying to argue, like, should we put ads in? Should we not put ads in it? And he says, look, he's like, you can't put ads in yet because it's not cool. Like, you got to be cool first, and then you can put ads in. So that's kind of my strategy. He's like, right now I'm trying to get really, really cool. I've done a couple where I'm like, because for me, it's like, whatever I'm doing right now, I'm talking about, like, hey, I just finished my coaching funnel. How many of you guys want it? Like, this is, I'll show it and the thing, like, do you want to go check it out? Go subscribe to the Loader. So I'll do it in a cool way like that. I'm not only doing ads. One of them, I did my morning routine. And I just wrote, like, a website URL, one of my favorite supplements on a card. I'm like, if you want the supplement, here it is. Like, show them the card. So it's like dorky things like that. And I'm sure eventually I'll have a consistent call to action, but not tell it's cool. And that's why I've got a lot of people in. So that's kind of my thoughts. I might be wrong, but. All right, so figuring out direct communication, that is the last step.
